rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND DASALLA VS. SF MUNI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion By To Have Requests For Admission Deemed Admitted And Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, September 14, 2018, Line 4, Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion By To Have Requests For Admission Deemed Admitted And Request For Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Aaron Minnis, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted, no opposition filed. The matters in Request for Admissions Nos. 1-4 are deemed admitted. CCP 233.280. Plaintiff Raymond DaSalla is ordered to pay defendant City and County of San Francisco sanctions in the amount of $750 within 30 days. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to aaron@minnisandsmallets.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, September 14, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT CAL WELLNESS' MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS The motion to quash service of summons by "Cal Wellness, an unincorporated, non-profit association" is DENIED. A registered process server declares under penalty of perjury that he personally served Cal Wellness via "David Sandersum" at "3225 W. Swain Rd., Stockton, CA 95219" on July 24, 2018 and also mailed the summons and complaint to that address. Nevin Eberhart-Sandstrom, declares that Cal Wellness has "never used" the Stockton address "as its business or mailing address." However, on February 3, 2017, Eberhardt-Sandstrom - acting as "president" of Cal Wellness - filed a general denial on its behalf that uses the address "3225 W. Swain Rd., Stockton, CA, 95219." At a minimum, substitute service was effected under CCP §415.20. Moreover, it is clear that Cal Wellness has notice of this suit. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Wellness is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ru

rulings.Case Title: LESLIE LAPAYOWKER VS. AIRBNB,INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, September 14, 2018, Line 2, PLAINTIFF LESLIE LAPAYOWKER Motion For Protective Order Pro Tem Judge Aaron Minnis, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: *Aaron Minnis would like to disclose that approximately two years ago he represented a client in a matter adverse to Airbnb which does not affect his ability to be impartial in this matter.* The motion is granted as to Siegel, Cune, Perry, Hendrickson, Evaldson, and Santa Fe Title Co. These subpoenas directed to out-of-state witnesses are facially invalid because a California subpoena for business records is not enforceable on an out-of-state non-party witness. See Amoco Chemical Co. v. Certain Underwriters at Lloyd's of London (1994) 34 Cal.App.4th 554, 559; Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 2020.220(c) ("Personal service of any deposition subpoena is effective to require all of the following of any deponent who is a resident of California at the time of service: . . . any specified production, inspection, testing, and sampling.") (emphasis added). The motion is granted, in part, as to Soestwohner, Oeding, Altman, and Brentview Medical Center. Plaintiff's medical records are private. However, the records are relevant to her claim that she suffered PTSD and severe psychological and emotional distress. Defendant has no other means to obtain information other than through plaintiff's medical records, although defendant's need for discovery does not automatically make discoverable plaintiff's entire medical history. Therefore, the subpoenas will be limited in scope to include records related to the mental conditions put at issue by plaintiff, and to exclude information that is either unrelated these conditions or pertain to plaintiff's past or present sexual practices or sexual conduct with individuals other than the defendant. Counsel for the parties are required to meet and confer to agree upon a protocol for handling these records, before they are produced, and ensuring compliance with this order. Sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to aaron@minnisandsmallets.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: OMAR SHAFIE VS. MEGHAN MCGARRY DANGANAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Renewed Motion To Compel Further Responses To Deposition Of Plaintiff Omar Shafie; Motion For Appointment Of Discovery Referee; Motion For Protective Order; Ccp 2025 480; Ccp 639; Request For Sanctions; / *** Notice Of Motion***
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, September 14, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANTS JOBE DANGANAN, MEGHAN MCGARRY'S Renewed Motion To Compel Further Responses To Deposition Of Plaintiff Omar Shafie; Motion For Appointment Of Discovery Referee; Motion For Protective Order; Ccp 2025 480; Ccp 639; Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Aaron Minnis, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted, in part. Defendants may take the deposition of plaintiff for an additional 3 hours on the record. The deposition shall take place within 20 days. Counsel shall comply with State Bar California Attorney Guidelines of Civility and Professionalism §9(a). The motion for protective order is granted as to plaintiff's special interrogatories, set three, to defendants because plaintiff has not met his burden of justifying that 268 special interrogatories are warranted, as required under CCP 2030.040; within 20 days defense counsel will send plaintiff's counsel the digital photos enabling plaintiff to determine the date of each photo for which this information is sought by way of the special interrogatories. Plaintiff's offer to temporarily cease its surveillance of defendants is incorporated into this order and will cease for 30 days. Request for sanctions is granted in the amount of $3,500 against plaintiff's counsel Mark Hooshmand payable to the firm of Tarkington, O'Neil, Barrack & Chong PC, within 20 days. The motion is otherwise denied. The request for discovery referee is denied without prejudice. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to aaron@minnisandsmallets.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TAMMY VANNORSDALL VS. ESPETUS CHURRASCARIA INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Tammy Vannorsdall Motion To Quash Subpoena Of Unemployment And Disability Records And Request For Sanctions Against Defendants And Their Counsel In The Amount Of $4,960.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, September 14, 2018, Line 9, Plaintiff Tammy Vannorsdall's Motion To Quash Subpoena Of Unemployment And Disability Records And Request For Sanctions Against Defendants And Their Counsel In The Amount Of $4,960. Defendants sought to subpoena plaintiff's "personal information" from the California Employment Development Department. (See Civ. Code §1798.3(a) ("'personal information' means any information that is maintained by an agency that identifies or describes an individual").) The EDD refused to produce the information. Defendants then failed to bring a motion to enforce the subpoena within 20 days of service of the EDD's written objection, rendering the subpoena moot. (See CCP §1985.3(g); CCP §1985.4 ("witness" defined as state agency; "consumer" includes state employees).) Given that the subpoena is moot, plaintiff's motion to quash it is also moot. No sanctions are awarded to anyone. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ru

rulings.Case Title: MARJORIE K VS. ANTHEM BLUE CROSS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Partial Joinder Of Defendant Maximus Federal Services, Inc. In Anthem Blue Cross'S Demurrer To Plaintiff'S Second Amended Complaint (Or In The Alternative Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, September 14, 2018, Line 10, Partial Joinder Of Defendant Maximus Federal Services, Inc. In Anthem Blue Cross'S Demurrer To Plaintiff'S Second Amended Complaint (Or In The Alternative Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings Anthem Blue Cross (partially joined by Maximus Federal Services, Inc.) demurs to the second amended complaint's first (breach of contract) and second (insurance bad faith) causes of action. It is adequately pled that insurance terms and coverages were misrepresented, claims and appeals mishandled and claims unreasonably denied. Marjorie K. and her son Preston may both sue so long as no double recovery results. (See, e.g., Laughner v. Bryne (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 904.) Marjorie has standing to sue as a party to the insurance contract and Preston because claims for his care were allegedly wrongfully denied. Preston is now an adult with the apparent legal capacity to sue, so the complaint may be so amended within 20 days. The demurrers are otherwise OVERRULED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ru

rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Blue Cross Of California Dba Anthem Blue Cross'S Motion To Strike Claims For And Reference To Punitive Damages And Attorney Fees From The Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, September 14, 2018, Line 10, Defendant Blue Cross Of California Dba Anthem Blue Cross' Motion To Strike Claims For And Reference To Punitive Damages And Attorney Fees From The Second Amended Complaint Anthem Blue Cross's motion to strike claimed punitive damages, treble damages and attorney fees is DENIED. It is adequately alleged that Anthem (1) acted fraudulently, maliciously, oppressively and in conscious disregard of rights, (2) violated Civil Code §3345 and (3) may be subject to an attorney fee award under, e.g., Brandt v.
